title: Would Third Impact
wikitext:
[]: Would Third Impact             Quote Post by Clover » Sat Nov 24, 2012 10:02 pm Change the world's climate? Could it disturb the earth's orbit? Is such a thing even possible?

[]:             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Sun Nov 25, 2012 2:56 am Yes, it would affect the Earth's climate, just as Second Impact did. No, it would not change the Earth's orbit in any way.

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Sun Nov 25, 2012 2:58 am Unless there was a massive explosion large enough to do so.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Nov 25, 2012 3:13 am Vegeta 20XX wrote: Yes, it would affect the Earth's climate, just as Second Impact did.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Nov 25, 2012 3:31 am Here is one I prepared earlier See this image [center] [/center] Now some people have looked in the foreground, and seen that now the trees have returned (having done their equivalent of imagining themselves in their own hearts), and so everything else is in good shape for everyone to come back and carry on as before, picking up from where they all left off. I looked at the background and saw a Giant Meteor Impact, without a Bruce Willis intervention. That splash implies that 100-mile-high Rei had managed to magic herself to some significant density, at least similar to that of flesh, enough that she packs the total energy of a mountain-size dinosaur killer, and, sprawling full length in the Pacific will cause significant tsunamis, if not also triggering tectonic activity around the whole Ring of Fire as the plates flex under her weight. Start by taking 160km/100 miles as a round number for Giant Naked Rei (GNR)'s height (though actually she looks taller than 12 geofront diameters), putting her centre of mass about 50 miles up — not enough that we have to worry about inverse square effects. Earth's escape velocity is 11.2km/s, which is the speed attained by falling one radius (4,000 miles) under 1g; and kinetic energy goes as velocity squared, so an asteroid with relative velocity 22.4km/s at infinity has the energy of a 16,000 mile fall under 1g; add in the potential energy from falling, that's a 20,000 mile fall or 400 times the energy per unit mass that GNR has. Assume GNR to have the density of water (or human flesh, much the same in bulk), while the asteroid is mainly silicate (density about 2.7 times that of water), then the asteroid has energy about 1000 times as much per unit volume. 1000 = 10³, so GNR will impact to zero altitude with the same total energy as an asteroid about one tenth her dimensions. The big assumption is about her density - but the splash image is clearly of something significantly denser than air; and Lilith's regeneration showed that she was not limited by anything quite so tawdry as classical conservation of stress-energy.

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Sun Nov 25, 2012 3:37 am ^Are you sure you prepared that? Good work.

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Sun Nov 25, 2012 12:52 pm Mr. Tines wrote: I looked at the background and saw a Giant Meteor Impact, without a Bruce Willis intervention. That splash implies that 100-mile-high Rei had managed to magic herself to some significant density, at least similar to that of flesh, enough that she packs the total energy of a mountain-size dinosaur killer, and, sprawling full length in the Pacific will cause significant tsunamis, if not also triggering tectonic activity around the whole Ring of Fire as the plates flex under her weight.

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Sun Nov 25, 2012 1:18 pm ^Incredible release of carbon into air=No sun/ incredible global warming Plunges world into darkness, heats world up a ridiculous amount. Either or.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Nov 25, 2012 1:51 pm Clover wrote: What would it do to the climate?

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Nov 25, 2012 2:48 pm What I don't understand is how such a massive addition of mass to Earth wouldn't screw with it's orbit... Could someone explain that to me, being as physics illiterate as I am? Isn't orbit based on gravity, which is based on mass? So if this huge mass just magicked onto the planet wouldn't it fuck with the orbit?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Nov 25, 2012 3:30 pm Adding somewhat less than 1/100,000,000 of its mass to the Earth will make negligible difference; even less than might be if as we observe she creates herself freely falling around the Sun as the Earth does.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Nov 25, 2012 5:13 pm Ah, so it's not that it wouldn't change it, it's just that it's so small by comparison it essentially wouldn't change it. I guess I'm just overestimating GNR's mass.

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Sun Nov 25, 2012 6:47 pm CJD wrote: Ah, so it's not that it wouldn't change it, it's just that it's so small by comparison it essentially wouldn't change it. I guess I'm just overestimating GNR's mass.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Nov 25, 2012 7:57 pm I'll note here, as I do every time this comes up, that Tines' assumptions don't seem to match up with the show's. We already know Anno doesn't pay much attention to actual real-life cause and effect; if he did he wouldn't have said 2I destroyed Antarctica (this would actually lower sea levels, since the ice pack would not offset the loss of land mass, and it would render the Earth a block of ice by 2015. There are lots of other problems as well, most of them tied to the oceanic conveyor and its impact on fisheries throughout the world). It's also clear that GNR's size and mass vary considerably throughout the movie, ranging from intangible to . . . whatever she is at the end. And of course, while we have that nifty head shot we also have the shot of Yui emerging from GNR's eye. The two do not match up, so which are we to take seriously (and would Anno give a shit either way)? Tines' math is great, but it's only useful if we're talking about a scenario with consistent moving parts. Since we're not . . .

[]:             Quote Post by Clover » Sun Nov 25, 2012 8:41 pm ayy

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Nov 25, 2012 9:13 pm Bagheera wrote: we also have the shot of Yui emerging from GNR's eye.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Nov 25, 2012 9:24 pm Reichu wrote: Hence, simplest explanation:

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Nov 25, 2012 10:54 pm The math is probably a wasted effort, yes, but those are still waves higher than mountains resulting from Rei's head falling in, and that's hardly the largest impact from the rest of her tumbling down too. Anno may totally handwave the details, but implication is still that the earth is largely fucked.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Nov 25, 2012 11:14 pm NemZ wrote: The math is probably a wasted effort, yes, but those are still waves higher than mountains resulting from Rei's head falling in, and that's hardly the largest impact from the rest of her tumbling down too. Anno may totally handwave the details, but implication is still that the earth is largely fucked.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Sun Nov 25, 2012 11:25 pm All else aside, if I were to hazard a guess, something as significant and meaning-changing as "and then the characters pretty much definitely died right afterwards" would probably have been depicted in a way not requiring mathematical calculations and years of forum wankery to determine.
summary:
[]: Would Third Impact             Quote Post by Clover » Sat Nov 24, 2012 10:02 pm Change the world's climate? Could it disturb the earth's orbit? Is such a thing even possible?

[]:             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Sun Nov 25, 2012 2:56 am Yes, it would affect the Earth's climate, just as Second Impact did. No, it would not change the Earth's orbit in any way.
